judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 hits all five expectations, including the application-level tenant_id filter as a second isolation layer; Report 2 covers the other four equally well but omits an explicit WHERE tenant_id defense-in-depth recommendation.

report_1.evidence: a table owner is exempt from that table's row-level-security policies by default — RLS is enforced against an owner only if the table also has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. The RLS migration db/migrations/0003_rls.sql only does ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY (line 1); it never issues FORCE.

report_2.evidence: In Postgres, ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY (line 1 of 0003_rls.sql) makes policies apply to ordinary roles but the table owner (and superusers) bypass RLS by default. To also subject the owner to policies you must additionally run ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY, which appears nowhere in the migrations.

report_1.evidence: Introduced by commit b2a23d2 ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", 2026-06-17). Before it, the runtime DATABASE_URL used the low-privilege juniper_app role... That commit changed the runtime credential from juniper_app to juniper_owner in three places — docker-compose.yml, .env.example, and infra/main.tf

report_2.evidence: commit b2a23d2 "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot" (Fixture Bot, 2026-06-17). That commit changed the runtime DATABASE_URL from the app role to the owner role in all three config surfaces: .env.example... docker-compose.yml... infra/main.tf:2

report_1.evidence: Primary fix: stop running the runtime application as the table owner. Point the runtime DATABASE_URL back to the least-privilege juniper_app role in docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:2.

report_2.evidence: Primary fix — stop running the request path as the table owner. Revert the runtime DATABASE_URL back to the low-privilege juniper_app role in docker-compose.yml, .env.example, and infra/main.tf:2.

report_1.evidence: Add an application-level WHERE tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid (or bind the claim tenant) in appointments.py:16-19, so isolation never depends on a single mechanism.

Boot-time / startup guardrail: on application start, assert the runtime DB role cannot bypass RLS — verify current_user is not the owner of the tenant tables and does not have BYPASSRLS/superuser; treat a failure as stop-the-line, not a warning to log.

report_2.evidence: Assert least privilege in CI/startup. On boot (or in a test), verify the runtime connection is not a table owner / has no DDL rights — e.g. check pg_roles/has_table_privilege and that the current user isn't the owner of appointments. Fail fast if the runtime role can bypass RLS.
